title: Foot bathing sheep for foot rot prevention
subtitle: Zinc sulphate foot baths: timing, concentration, and when they help versus when they don't.
excerpt: A foot bath with zinc sulphate solution is the most practical herd-level treatment for foot scald and as a barrier to foot rot in wet conditions. Used correctly it hardens the soft skin between the toes and reduces infection pressure on the whole flock. Used incorrectly it delays the trimming and individual treatment that infected feet actually need.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Five Point Lameness Plan (open-access, https://ahdb.org.uk). DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep, Crown copyright. SAC Consulting Scotland, sheep foot care guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bacterial infection of the foot caused by Dichelobacter nodosus, causing softening, separation, and severe lameness. Highly contagious and spreads rapidly in wet conditions.
A milder bacterial infection of the soft skin between the toes, causing redness and tenderness without the separation of true foot rot. Responsive to zinc sulphate foot bathing.
A mineral compound used in livestock foot baths at a 10 percent concentration. It hardens the skin between the toes and has bactericidal properties against the organisms that cause foot scald.

content.text: Foot bathing is a preventive and early-treatment tool, not a cure for established
foot rot
. Sheep with full-thickness foot rot need trimming and targeted antibiotic treatment. A foot bath will not resolve an already-infected foot. Its value is in protecting healthy feet and clearing mild
foot scald
before it progresses.
When to use a foot bath

Autumn and early spring are the highest-risk periods, when ground is consistently wet and pasture rotation puts sheep through the same gateways repeatedly. Run the flock through a foot bath at the start of a wet period and again after four to five days if the ground stays wet.
Foot bathing is also useful after buying in new stock. New arrivals may carry
foot rot
organisms even when showing no symptoms. Running them through a zinc bath on arrival reduces the risk of introducing infection to the resident flock.
Making up the solution
Use
zinc sulphate

at 10 percent concentration: 1 kg of zinc sulphate dissolved in 10 litres of water. A standard drive-through trough holds around 60 litres, so mix 6 kg of zinc sulphate per filling. Stir thoroughly before the flock enters; the salt settles rapidly and a weak top layer defeats the purpose.

The solution stays active for one to two days. Once the bath turns brown from accumulated dirt and faeces, it is no longer effective. Discard it and refill. A heavily soiled bath can carry infection from one sheep to the next.

attrs.title: Formalin is no longer recommended
attrs.body: Formalin (formaldehyde solution) was the traditional active agent in sheep foot baths but is now known to be a carcinogen and its use in livestock is no longer recommended by the Five Point Lameness Plan. Zinc sulphate is safer to handle and effective. Do not use formalin.

content.text: Running the flock through

Place the trough at a gateway or at the exit to a race where sheep walk in single file. The trough should be at least 2 metres long so each foot is in solution for 30 to 60 seconds. Sheep need to walk, not jump; a trough that is too short results in most animals clearing it in one stride.

Stand sheep in the bath for one to two minutes where possible. Standing produces more contact than walking through. Set up a small pen on the exit side so the batch stands waiting while the next group enters. After bathing, hold the flock on a dry concrete yard or clean dry pasture for at least 30 minutes before turning back onto wet ground.
Identifying lame sheep before the bath
Walk the flock at a steady pace before bathing. Any sheep showing three-legged lameness or sitting down to eat should be caught and examined before going through the bath. Badly infected feet need
hoof trimming
and antibiotic injection, not a foot bath. Running a severely lame sheep through zinc sulphate delays proper treatment.

items.symptom: Lameness rates not dropping after two rounds of foot bathing
items.cause: Established foot rot in the flock that needs individual treatment, or the bath concentration is too low
items.fix: Check the concentration of the solution. Identify lame sheep and treat with antibiotic and trimming rather than repeated bathing. Contact your vet for a flock-level treatment plan.

items.symptom: Bath solution turns murky grey or brown before the flock is through
items.cause: Too much faecal contamination; sheep soiling in the trough
items.fix: Drive sheep through in smaller groups. Discard and refill after 30 to 40 sheep or when the solution discolours visibly.

items.symptom: Sheep jumping the trough rather than walking through
items.cause: Trough sides too low, or sheep conditioned to avoid it
items.fix: Add boards to extend the height of the trough sides. Slow the sheep down with a narrow race leading in. Some flocks take two or three passes before they stop trying to clear the trough.
